did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses AI methods including YOLO and SAM architectures for melanoma detection and segmentation in dermoscopic images, and a pipeline with transfer learning, feature extraction (LBP), and machine learning classifiers (MLP) for classification. It also employs a Large Language Model (LLM) for generating pre-diagnostic text assistance.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new approach for melanoma detection, segmentation, and classification using YOLO, SAM, and LLM architectures. It details specific methods (transfer learning, feature extraction, grid search), reports original quantitative results (99% accuracy for detection, 86.0% for classification) on the PH2 dataset, and includes a 'Results and Discussion' section with statistical analysis (Bartlett's test), distinguishing it from a review or survey.

title: New approach Generative AI Melanoma Data Fusion for classification in dermoscopic images with Large Language Model
authors: Carlos Mauricio Jaborandy de Mattos Dourado Junior
journal: 2024 37th SIBGRAPI Conference on Graphics, Patterns and Images (SIBGRAPI)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel approach for melanoma detection, segmentation, and classification in dermoscopic images using a combination of YOLO and SAM architectures alongside a Large Language Model (LLM) for pre-diagnostic assistance. The authors utilized the PH2 and ISIC 2017 datasets to train and evaluate their pipeline, which integrates transfer learning, feature extraction (LBP, VGG, DenseNet), and machine learning classifiers (MLP, KNN, SVM, etc.). The detection and segmentation phase achieved an accuracy of 99% using the SAM + YOLOv8n model, while the classification phase reached 86.0% accuracy with the LBP-MLP combination. The system generates textual pre-diagnoses by fusing extracted medical attributes with LLM outputs. The results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms several existing studies in the literature for melanoma analysis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the detection, segmentation, and classification of melanoma in dermoscopic images. The classification task specifically distinguishes between benign and malignant melanoma (malignancy classification). The PH2 dataset was used for detection/segmentation, and the ISIC 2017 dataset was used for classification.

The classification pipeline distinguishes melanoma as 'benign' or 'malignant'. The introduction mentions 'simple moles or warts' as conditions distinguished from melanoma by ABCDE characteristics, and the classification task involves identifying benign cases within the ISIC 2017 dataset context.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses YOLO architectures to detect melanoma regions and produce bounding boxes in dermoscopic images, achieving 99% accuracy.

The paper uses SAM (Segment Anything Model) combined with YOLO to generate refined binary segmentation masks for melanoma lesions, reporting metrics such as Jaccard Index, Dice coefficient, Sensitivity, and Specificity.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy status (Benign vs. Malignant) and medical attributes
all_task_types.details: The primary classification task distinguishes between benign and malignant melanoma using a pipeline of feature extractors (LBP, VGG, DenseNet) and classifiers (MLP, KNN, SVM, etc.), achieving 86.0% accuracy. It also classifies medical attributes such as anatomical site and approximate patient age.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public database for the analysis of dermoscopic images provided by the dermatology service of Hospital Pedro Hispano in Portugal.

use.training: Used for training the YOLO and SAM models for melanoma detection and segmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation via cross-validation to generate means and deviations for segmentation metrics.
use.testing: Used for testing segmentation performance (comparison with gold standard).
use.other: Used for comparison with state-of-the-art methods for segmentation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: Part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration Archive, used in 2017 for the melanoma detection challenge, including clinical information about the patient and the lesion.

use.training: Used for training the classification pipeline (feature extraction and classifier selection).
use.validation: Used for validation via cross-validation to select the best extractor-classifier combination.
use.testing: Used for testing the classification accuracy of the proposed pipeline.
use.other: Used for classification of malignancy and extraction of medical attributes (age, anatomical site, size).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess statistical equality or difference between the result vectors of the best segmentation model (SAM+YOLOv8n) and other segmentation models.
To validate results and generate means and standard deviations for segmentation and classification metrics.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The result vectors of the best segmentation model (SAM+YOLOv8n) and the comparator models have equal variances (statistical equality).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The result vectors of the best segmentation model (SAM+YOLOv8n) and the comparator models have unequal variances (statistical difference).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The test indicated statistical equality between the SAM+YOLOv8n model and the SAM+YOLOv8m, SAM+YOLOv8s, and SAM+YOLOv9c models, and statistical difference for the SAM+YOLOv8l, SAM+YOLOv8x, and SAM+YOLOv9e models.

PH2.split: Test (implied by comparison with gold standard)
PH2.notes: The SAM + YOLOv8n model was identified as the best performing model. Bartlett's test indicated statistical equality between SAM+YOLOv8n and SAM+YOLOv8m, SAM+YOLOv8s, and SAM+YOLOv9c. Statistical difference was found for SAM+YOLOv8l, SAM+YOLOv8x, and SAM+YOLOv9e. The proposed method (SAM+YOLOv8n) achieved state-of-the-art performance compared to Deep class (2019), Geodesic (2019), and FLog Parzen (2020) on the PH2 dataset.

ISIC 2017.notes: The best combination for classification was LBP feature extractor with MLP classifier, achieving 86.0% accuracy. The system showed low accuracy for classifying lesion location and patient age, requiring context beyond the melanoma region. The LBP extractor outperformed CNN extractors (DenseNet, VGG) likely due to texture variability in melanomas.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-stage pipeline: YOLO (You Only Look Once) for detection, SAM (Segment Anything Model) for segmentation, and a feature extraction/classification pipeline (LBP, VGG16, DenseNet121 + MLP, KNN, NaiveBayes, RandomForest, SVM) for classification, integrated with a Large Language Model (LLM) for pre-diagnostic text generation.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was used for the classification pipeline. YOLO models were trained on the PH2 dataset. SAM is a pre-trained segmentation framework developed by META AI. Specific pretraining details for the LLM are not reported.

architecture_details.detection_model: YOLOv8l, YOLOv8m, YOLOv8n, YOLOv8s, YOLOv8x, YOLOv9c, YOLOv9e
architecture_details.segmentation_model: SAM (Segment Anything Model) initialized with YOLO bounding boxes and binary masks, combined via intersection.
classifiers: SVM (Support Vector Machine) - Linear, Polynomial, RBF
architecture_details.llm_component: Large Language Model used for generating textual pre-diagnosis by fusing extracted medical attributes (malignancy, size, age, anatomical site) with clinical context.

training_details.optimization_method: Grid search used to optimize hyperparameters of classifiers.
training_details.validation_method: Cross-validation employed to validate results and generate means and standard deviations.

loss_functions.SAM_segmentation: L_seg = λ_IoU (1 - IoU) + λ_BCE (-1/N Σ (y_i log(ŷ_i) + (1-y_i) log(1-ŷ_i)))

hyperparameters.SAM_loss_weights: λ_IoU and λ_BCE (values not specified, described as controlling relative importance)
hyperparameters.classifier_hyperparameters: Optimized via grid search (specific values not reported)

classification_or_output_head.detection_output: Bounding boxes and binary masks for melanoma regions.
classification_or_output_head.classification_output: Binary classification (Benign/Malignant) and regression/classification of medical attributes (clinical size, anatomical site, approximate age).

preprocessing_techniques: Data fusion: Combining binary detection masks from YOLO and SAM frameworks via intersection to refine segmentation edges.
Transfer learning: Applied to the classification pipeline for feature extraction and classification.
Grid search: Used to optimize hyperparameters of classifiers.
Cross-validation: Employed to validate results and generate means and standard deviations for metrics.
conclusions: The proposed multi-stage approach combining YOLO and SAM architectures for melanoma detection and segmentation achieved an accuracy of 99% (specifically 99.13% ± 0.40% with SAM + YOLOv8n), surpassing state-of-the-art methods on the PH2 dataset.

The SAM + YOLOv8n model demonstrated statistical equality in performance with SAM + YOLOv8m, SAM + YOLOv8s, and SAM + YOLOv9c models, but statistical difference against heavier models like SAM + YOLOv8l, SAM + YOLOv8x, and SAM + YOLOv9e.

The classification pipeline, utilizing a combination of feature extraction (LBP, VGG16, DenseNet121) and classifiers (MLP, KNN, NaiveBayes, RandomForest, SVM), achieved a maximum accuracy of 86.0% ± 1.0% using the LBP feature extractor with an MLP classifier on the ISIC 2017 dataset.
The LBP feature extractor outperformed CNN-based extractors (DenseNet121 and VGG16) in classification tasks, likely due to the texture variability inherent in melanoma images.

The system effectively classified malignancy, lesion diameter, and diagnosis confirmation type, but showed lower accuracy for classifying anatomical site and patient age, indicating a need for broader clinical context beyond the lesion region.

The integration of a Large Language Model (LLM) successfully generated textual pre-diagnostic reports by fusing extracted medical attributes (malignancy, size, age, anatomical site) with clinical context.
The proposed classification method achieved state-of-the-art performance on the ISIC 2017 dataset, with an accuracy 4.71% higher than the second-best method (Al et al.).
The study demonstrates that a pipeline integrating transfer learning, data fusion, and generative AI can effectively assist in the pre-diagnosis of melanoma in dermoscopic images.
important_remarks: The study relies on two specific public datasets (PH2 for detection/segmentation and ISIC 2017 for classification) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability.

The classification performance is modest (86.0% accuracy) with notably low recall (50.0%) and F1-score (47.0%) for the LBP-MLP model, suggesting a high rate of false negatives which is critical in melanoma diagnosis.

The LBP feature extractor outperformed deep learning extractors (DenseNet121, VGG16) in classification, which is counter-intuitive and may indicate overfitting or insufficient data for deep feature learning in this specific pipeline.
The system showed poor performance in classifying anatomical site and patient age, indicating that the model lacks the ability to infer broader clinical context beyond the lesion region.

The segmentation results for heavier YOLO models (e.g., YOLOv8l, YOLOv8x) were statistically worse than lighter models (YOLOv8n), potentially due to the small size of the PH2 dataset (200 images) being insufficient for robustly training larger architectures.

The integration of a Large Language Model (LLM) for pre-diagnostic text generation is a novel contribution, but the quality and clinical safety of these generated texts are not rigorously evaluated by medical experts.

Data leakage risks exist if the cross-validation splits were not strictly patient-level, as images from the same patient might appear in both training and testing sets, though the paper does not explicitly confirm patient-level splitting.

The paper claims state-of-the-art performance but compares against older or less direct baselines; the comparison with recent deep learning methods on ISIC 2017 is limited to accuracy metrics without detailed confusion matrices or AUC scores.
